content.assessment: Reforming admissions mechanisms while preserving track structure and geography does not alter the spatial concentration of selective schools or the cohort contraction dynamic; it operates on a different causal channel (who gets selected) rather than where/how many sorting points exist. Its interaction with demographic trends is largely neutral: it neither exacerbates nor resolves the urban-rural distributional imbalance, though it could marginally affect who benefits from a shrinking pool of coveted urban seats.
opportunities: Could be paired with cohort-driven consolidation planning to ensure that reformed admissions criteria apply to a stable defined set of surviving schools
strengths: Does not require restructuring the geographic network of schools, so it is compatible with ongoing consolidation pressures without adding disruption
Precedent of temporary segregation reduction from 2000s administrative measures shows admissions-level reform is at least technically capable of shifting outcomes independent of enrollment trends
threats: Reform fatigue and stagnation precedent from the 2000s suggests admissions-only reform may not survive the ongoing multi-year contraction pressure that is reshaping the school network anyway
Divided union positions could delay implementation past a window where enrollment decline forces closures regardless of any admissions redesign
weaknesses: Does not address the structural insulation of urban/selective tracks from rural depopulation; village students (only 3% of gimnazium locations) remain geographically excluded regardless of admissions design
Church-maintained and private schools sit outside central authority, meaning coverage gaps could persist unevenly across the shrinking cohort landscape
